response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","opinion":"Honestly, talking about all this health stuff—the vaccines, the whole pandemic thing—it just feels exhausting sometimes. Like my brain gets overloaded just trying to keep up with all the different takes online. I spend so much time scrolling through social media, and it's like everyone has a strong opinion on everything, and they’re not really listening to each other. It makes me feel kind of overwhelmed and even depressed when I see all the arguments flying around, especially about something as serious as health. Because I know that my friends and family are worried, and I care about them so much, but sometimes the debates spiral into just name-calling or getting overly aggressive, and it’s impossible to actually have a civil conversation. 

If I had to give some advice, especially when talking to people in person—like at a salon, or with friends—I think the biggest thing is setting boundaries before it even starts. It's okay to say, 'Hey, this is a really heavy topic right now, and honestly, I'm not in a place where I can debate policy, but I do want to hear how you’re doing.' Because my low extraversion means that big confrontations drain me instantly, and arguing just makes me feel worse. Instead of trying to prove who is 'right,' which is impossible anyway because science is always changing and people interpret it differently, I try to shift the focus back to shared human experience. Like, instead of discussing efficacy rates, we can talk about how hard it was for us to get through lockdown, or what little things made us feel normal again—maybe a nice walk in the park when the birds were singing, or just binge-watching a show with friends. 

When I do have to address the topic, especially online, I try to remember that most people are operating from a place of fear or deep concern for their loved ones. They aren't necessarily trying to be antagonistic; they’re scared. So, instead of engaging with the extreme claims—the 'you must believe this' kind of posts—I focus on validating the *feeling* behind the post. I might say something like, 'It sounds like you are really worried about your parents' safety,' rather than arguing about vaccine side effects. That way, I acknowledge their fear without having to argue facts, which is where I tend to lose it. It’s a constant balancing act of protecting my own mental space while still trying to be empathetic, and honestly, that takes more energy than doing my hair for a full day's worth of clients sometimes.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Honestly, talking about all this health stuff—the vaccines, the whole pandemic thing—it just feels exhausting sometimes. Like my brain gets overloaded just trying to keep up with all the different takes online. I spend so much time scrolling through social media, and it's like everyone has a strong opinion on everything, and they’re not really listening to each other. It makes me feel kind of overwhelmed and even depressed when I see all the arguments flying around, especially about something as serious as health. Because I know that my friends and family are worried, and I care about them so much, but sometimes the debates spiral into just name-calling or getting overly aggressive, and it’s impossible to actually have a civil conversation. 

If I had to give some advice, especially when talking to people in person—like at a salon, or with friends—I think the biggest thing is setting boundaries before it even starts. It's okay to say, 'Hey, this is a really heavy topic right now, and honestly, I'm not in a place where I can debate policy, but I do want to hear how you’re doing.' Because my low extraversion means that big confrontations drain me instantly, and arguing just makes me feel worse. Instead of trying to prove who is 'right,' which is impossible anyway because science is always changing and people interpret it differently, I try to shift the focus back to shared human experience. Like, instead of discussing efficacy rates, we can talk about how hard it was for us to get through lockdown, or what little things made us feel normal again—maybe a nice walk in the park when the birds were singing, or just binge-watching a show with friends. 

When I do have to address the topic, especially online, I try to remember that most people are operating from a place of fear or deep concern for their loved ones. They aren't necessarily trying to be antagonistic; they’re scared. So, instead of engaging with the extreme claims—the 'you must believe this' kind of posts—I focus on validating the *feeling* behind the post. I might say something like, 'It sounds like you are really worried about your parents' safety,' rather than arguing about vaccine side effects. That way, I acknowledge their fear without having to argue facts, which is where I tend to lose it. It’s a constant balancing act of protecting my own mental space while still trying to be empathetic, and honestly, that takes more energy than doing my hair for a full day's worth of clients sometimes.
